title: Reverse Bits
description:
Reverse bits of a given 32 bits unsigned integer.

Note that in some languages, such as Java, there is no unsigned integer type. In this case, both input and output will be given as a signed integer type. They should not affect your implementation, as the integer's internal binary representation is the same, whether it is signed or unsigned.
whatShouldHappen: Iterate through all 32 bits of the input integer
Extract the rightmost bit of the input and append it to the result
Shift the result to the left and the input to the right in each iteration
skeletonHint: Use a loop that runs 32 times. In each iteration, take the last bit of `n` using `n & 1`, add it to your result, then shift `n` to the right and your result to the left. Be careful with signed/unsigned shifts in JavaScript (`>>>` is your friend).
patternExplanation: The problem requires manipulating individual bits of an integer. Shifting and bitwise OR/AND operations are the primary tools for this.

title: Number of 1 Bits
description: Write a function that takes the binary representation of an unsigned integer and returns the number of '1' bits it has (also known as the Hamming weight).
whatShouldHappen: Count the number of set bits (1s) in the integer
Process the integer bit by bit or use a bit manipulation trick
skeletonHint: You can either check each of the 32 bits by shifting and masking, or use a clever trick: `n & (n - 1)` always removes the rightmost set bit. How many times can you do that until `n` becomes 0?
patternExplanation: Efficiently counting bits involves bitwise operations. Brian Kernighan's algorithm is a classic way to count set bits by repeatedly stripping the lowest bit.

title: Sum of Two Integers
description: Given two integers `a` and `b`, return *the sum of the two integers without using the operators* `+` and `-`.
whatShouldHappen: Calculate sum using bitwise XOR for addition
Use bitwise AND and shift left for carry logic
Iterate until there is no carry left
skeletonHint: Addition can be broken into two parts: the sum without carry and the carry itself. XOR (`^`) gives the sum without carry, and AND (`&`) shifted left (`<< 1`) gives the carry. Repeat the process until the carry is 0.
patternExplanation: The problem specifically forbids standard arithmetic operators, requiring bitwise logic to simulate a half-adder or full-adder circuit.

title: Counting Bits
description: Given an integer `n`, return *an array* `ans` *of length* `n + 1` *such that for each* `i` (`0 <= i <= n`), `ans[i]` *is the **number of 1's** in the binary representation of* `i`.
whatShouldHappen: Generate an array of size n + 1
Count set bits for every number from 0 to n
Optimize using dynamic programming based on previous bit counts
skeletonHint: While you could solve this by calling a 'popcount' function for each number, there's a DP relationship. The number of bits in `i` is the same as the number of bits in `i / 2` (shifted right) plus 1 if `i` is odd.
patternExplanation: This problem combines bit manipulation with dynamic programming. The count of bits for a number can be derived from the count of bits for a smaller number.

title: Missing Number
description: Given an array `nums` containing `n` distinct numbers in the range `[0, n]`, return *the only number in the range that is missing from the array*.
whatShouldHappen: Compute the XOR sum of all numbers from 0 to n
Compute the XOR sum of all numbers in the input array
XOR the two results to find the missing number
skeletonHint: You can use the formula for the sum of the first n numbers (n*(n+1)/2) and subtract the sum of the array, or use bitwise XOR. XORing a number with itself cancels it out, so XORing all expected and actual numbers will leave only the missing one.
patternExplanation: XOR is ideal here because it's commutative and associative, and x ^ x = 0. By XORing every index and every value, all pairs cancel out except for the missing number.
